Server-Sent Events (SSE) is a one-way server-to-client stream over a single long-lived HTTP response with built-in auto-reconnect and event IDs — ideal for notifications, feeds, or streaming LLM tokens where the client only consumes. WebSockets upgrade the connection to a full-duplex bidirectional channel, best for chat, collaborative editing, or games where the client also pushes frequently. Pick SSE for simple server-push (it's cheaper and reconnects for free), WebSockets when you genuinely need two-way low-latency, and long polling only as a fallback.
fs: Long poll = universal fallback; SSE = one-way server push (auto-reconnect); WebSocket = full-duplex two-way.
fj: Reaches for WebSockets for one-way notifications that SSE would handle more cheaply.

followups.q: How does SSE's Last-Event-ID make reconnection resume cleanly?
followups.a: The server tags each event with an `id:`; on a dropped connection the browser's `EventSource` automatically reconnects and sends the last id back in the `Last-Event-ID` header. The server can then replay events the client missed, giving at-least-once continuity without custom code. WebSockets have no built-in equivalent — you'd implement reconnect-and-resume yourself.

followups.q: What's a key operational limitation of SSE over HTTP/1.1, and how is it mitigated?
followups.a: Over HTTP/1.1 browsers cap concurrent connections per origin (~6), and each SSE stream holds one open — so a few tabs/streams exhaust the budget and block other requests. HTTP/2 multiplexing removes this by sharing one connection across many streams. So SSE at scale effectively wants HTTP/2; on HTTP/1.1 you must budget connections carefully.

followups.q: Why don't WebSockets get HTTP caching, compression, or proxy handling for free like SSE?
followups.a: SSE rides a normal HTTP response, so it inherits standard infrastructure — proxies, gzip, auth headers, reconnection — and degrades gracefully. WebSockets `Upgrade` to a non-HTTP framed protocol, so HTTP-aware proxies/CDNs may not understand it, and you reimplement reconnection, heartbeats, and backpressure yourself. That plumbing is the cost of full-duplex; don't pay it for one-way push.

q: Why offer bulk/batch endpoints, and what are the design hazards?
a: Bulk endpoints let a client send many operations in one request (e.g. POST /orders/batch with an array, or bulk import), cutting round-trip overhead and per-request auth/rate-limit cost versus N separate calls. The hazards: partial failure — decide whether the batch is all-or-nothing (transactional) or per-item, and if per-item, return a structured per-item result with index, status, and error so the client knows exactly what failed. Cap the batch size to bound memory and latency, and apply rate limits by item count, not request count, so one giant batch can't bypass throttling. For very large or slow jobs, make it asynchronous: accept the batch with 202, return a job id, and let the client poll for progress. Keep each item idempotent so retrying a partially-applied batch is safe.
fs: Batch cuts round trips; decide all-or-nothing vs per-item results, cap size, throttle by items, go async (202) when large.
fj: Returns one overall 200/500 for a 10k-item batch with no per-item status, so clients can't tell what applied.

followups.q: What HTTP status should a partial-success batch return, and why is 200 defensible here?
followups.a: When items individually succeed or fail, the *batch request itself* succeeded, so `200` (or `207 Multi-Status`, WebDAV-style) with per-item statuses in the body fits — the transport worked. A blanket `4xx`/`5xx` would wrongly imply nothing applied. The per-item `status` fields carry the real outcomes; the top-level code reflects whether the batch was accepted, not whether every item passed.

followups.q: Why rate-limit by item count rather than request count for batch endpoints?
followups.a: If you throttle per *request*, a client can pack 10,000 operations into one call and bypass a 'N requests/min' limit entirely — the batch becomes a throttling loophole. Counting *items* (or weighting cost by payload size) makes one big batch consume proportional quota, so a batch of 1000 costs ~1000× a single op. The limit should track the real work, which lives in items, not requests.

followups.q: Why must each item be idempotent for a batch to be safely retryable?
followups.a: If a batch half-applies then the connection drops, the client retries the *whole* batch — re-sending items that already succeeded. Without per-item idempotency (keys or natural upserts), the succeeded items get duplicated. Idempotent items mean re-processing the already-applied ones is a no-op, so retrying a partially-applied batch converges correctly instead of double-creating records.

q: What is contract testing, and how does an OpenAPI spec reduce integration breakage?
a: An OpenAPI spec is a machine-readable description of your API's paths, request/response schemas, and status codes; treated as the source of truth it drives code generation, request/response validation, mock servers, and docs, so producer and consumer share one definition. Contract testing verifies that the API a provider exposes still satisfies what consumers actually depend on — without spinning up the full system. Consumer-driven contract testing (e.g. Pact) records the requests a consumer makes and the responses it expects, then replays them against the provider in CI so a provider change that would break a consumer fails the build before deploy. Combined with schema validation against the OpenAPI spec, this catches breaking changes (removed fields, type changes) at PR time rather than in production.
fs: OpenAPI = shared source of truth (gen/validate/mock); contract tests (Pact) fail CI when a provider breaks a consumer.
fj: Relies on hand-written docs and discovers breaking changes only when a downstream service errors in prod.

followups.q: How does consumer-driven contract testing differ from just validating against the OpenAPI spec?
followups.a: OpenAPI validation checks a response conforms to the *full published schema* — but a provider can stay schema-valid while removing a field *some consumer relies on*. Consumer-driven contracts (Pact) capture only what *each real consumer* uses, so CI fails specifically when a change breaks an actual dependency. It tests 'do I break my consumers,' not just 'do I match my own spec.'

followups.q: Why is spec-first (write OpenAPI before code) often preferred over generating the spec from code?
followups.a: Spec-first makes the contract a deliberate, reviewable artifact agreed before implementation, so consumers can start mocking against it in parallel and breaking changes show up as spec diffs in review. Code-first generation risks the spec drifting to whatever the code happens to do, encoding accidental behavior as contract. Spec-first treats the API as a designed interface, not a byproduct.

followups.q: What breaking changes can contract testing miss?
followups.a: It mostly catches *structural* breaks (removed fields, type changes) on paths consumers exercise. It can miss *semantic* changes — a field's meaning shifting (dollars→cents), tightened input validation, altered ordering — since the shape still matches. It also only covers consumers that *have* contracts; unknown clients aren't represented. Pair it with versioning and deprecation discipline.

q: What is backpressure in an API, and how do you apply it without dropping work?
a: Backpressure is the signal a system sends upstream when it can't keep up, so producers slow down instead of overwhelming a consumer and causing unbounded queue growth, memory blowups, and latency collapse. At the API edge you apply it explicitly: rate limit and return 429 with Retry-After, bound concurrency with admission control / a semaphore and shed load with 503 when saturated, and cap request and queue sizes rather than buffering without limit. For streaming protocols, the transport helps — HTTP/2 and gRPC have flow-control windows, and reactive frameworks let a slow consumer request N items at a time. The anti-pattern is an unbounded in-memory queue that absorbs a spike until the process OOMs; bounded queues plus a clear 429/503 signal let clients back off and retry with jitter.
fs: Backpressure = tell producers to slow down: 429/Retry-After, bounded queues, admission control/503, flow control on streams.
fj: Absorbs every spike into an unbounded in-memory queue until the service runs out of memory.

followups.q: Why is shedding load with 503 sometimes BETTER than queuing everything?
followups.a: An unbounded queue under sustained overload grows latency without bound — requests sit so long they've already timed out when served, wasting work and eventually OOMing. Shedding with `503`/`429` keeps the served subset fast and lets clients back off, preserving throughput. Queuing only helps *transient* bursts; for sustained overload, fast rejection beats slow-then-crash.

followups.q: How does HTTP/2 / gRPC flow control provide backpressure at the transport layer?
followups.a: Each stream has a receive *window*; the receiver advertises how many bytes it's ready to accept, and the sender must stop once the window is exhausted until the receiver acks more. A slow consumer simply stops growing its window, automatically throttling the producer at the protocol level — no application code needed. This is true backpressure: the slow side controls the fast side's send rate.

followups.q: What's the 'bufferbloat' analogy for why big buffers hurt latency under load?
followups.a: Like an oversized network buffer that fills and adds huge queuing delay instead of signaling congestion, a large app queue absorbs a backlog that becomes latency — every queued request waits behind the whole backlog. Small bounded buffers fail fast and surface pressure early (`429`/`503`) so producers slow down. Bigger buffers trade visible rejection for invisible, growing delay.

q: How do you design a reliable webhook delivery system on the provider side?
a: Decouple event production from delivery: when an event occurs, persist it durably and hand delivery to a queue/worker rather than POSTing inline on the request path. Each attempt POSTs the signed payload (HMAC over the raw body plus a timestamp) and treats only a fast 2xx as success; on non-2xx or timeout, retry with exponential backoff and jitter over a bounded window (e.g. hours), then mark it failed and surface it. Make delivery idempotent from the consumer's side by sending a stable event id so duplicates are safe, since you guarantee at-least-once. Add a per-endpoint circuit breaker so a consistently-failing customer endpoint doesn't starve workers, and give customers a dashboard to inspect, replay, and rotate signing secrets. A short request timeout per attempt keeps one slow consumer from blocking the pool.
fs: Persist event, deliver async via queue, sign (HMAC+timestamp), retry w/ backoff+jitter, stable event id, per-endpoint breaker.
fj: POSTs the webhook synchronously in the originating request and gives up after one failed attempt.

followups.q: Why is a per-endpoint circuit breaker essential when one customer's endpoint is down?
followups.a: Without it, a dead customer endpoint keeps consuming worker threads and retry slots — each attempt waits out the timeout — starving delivery to every *healthy* customer (noisy-neighbor failure). A per-endpoint breaker trips after repeated failures, parking that customer's deliveries so workers stay free for everyone else. It isolates one bad consumer from the whole fleet's delivery.

followups.q: How should the provider support secret rotation without dropping in-flight deliveries?
followups.a: Allow two active signing secrets per endpoint during rotation, sending signatures for both secrets in the header. The customer verifies against either until they've fully cut over, then you retire the old secret. A hard single-secret swap would fail verification for any request signed before the customer updated — overlap makes rotation zero-downtime.

followups.q: What's the right move when retries exhaust the bounded window — drop, or something else?
followups.a: Don't silently drop: mark the event failed, move it to a dead-letter store, and surface it in the customer dashboard with a manual *replay* option so they recover after fixing their endpoint. Optionally alert them. Silent loss means the consumer's state diverges from yours; a DLQ plus replay turns exhausted retries into a recoverable, visible state, not data loss.
